Party_Details.Name: Long Say Ting Daniel

Facts.Text: From July 2005, Daniel Long Say Ting worked as a Mercedes limousine driver for Dr Jusuf Merukh during the latter's business trips to Singapore.
From June 2007, Daniel Long assisted Dr Jusuf Merukh in sourcing for and buying condominium units in Singapore.
On 30 April 2010, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd was incorporated with Daniel Long and Dr Jusuf Merukh as directors and Dr Jusuf Merukh as sole shareholder.
On 30 April 2010, Daniel Long became a director of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
By 30 April 2010, Daniel Long had incorporated a total of 14 Singapore companies wholly owned by Dr Jusuf Merukh.
From 30 April 2010, the business of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd was described as an investment company.
Between 30 April 2010 and 30 April 2011, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd incurred expenses of $1,424,377.00, mostly for acquiring properties, and drew no income.
All acquisitions made by Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd up to 30 April 2011 were funded through transfers from Dr Jusuf Merukh’s bank account in Indonesia.

By 30 April 2010, Dr Jusuf Merukh had given Daniel Long a Power of Attorney to manage and carry out his business transactions and to act as local representative for his business ventures in Singapore.
By 30 April 2010, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd had embarked on the purchase of seven additional condominium units, which were later not completed due to fund transfer problems from Indonesia.

By 30 April 2010, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd owned three properties: the Bayshore property at Blk 72 Bayshore Road #29-16 Costa Del Sol, the Kitchener property at 10 Kitchener Link #13-18 City Square Residences, and The Raintree at 87 Bukit Drive #06-18 The Raintree.

By 30 April 2010, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd paid for rental of the church attended by Dr Jusuf Merukh in Singapore and purchased a fleet of Rolls Royce, BMW and Mercedes Benz motorcars with chauffeurs for use by his family, staff, business associates and church pastors.
By 30 April 2010, Daniel Long was working as a full-time local director for Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd despite having no prior business experience.
By 30 April 2010, Daniel Long was a co-local director of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd together with Tan Kim Huat and was a personal guarantor for Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd’s debts.
On 22 June 2011, Dr Jusuf Merukh died, after which Daniel Long remained as the sole director of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
After 22 June 2011, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd fell behind on staff salaries and monthly loan instalments for the Three Properties.
On 27 June 2011, Daniel Long sent a text message to Rudolf Johanes Merukh requesting a meeting to discuss Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd’s operations and properties.
On 5 July 2011, Daniel Long and Rudolf Johanes Merukh had a phone call and text exchange about the financial status and obligations of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.

On 5 July 2011, Daniel Long emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh an update listing operating costs and outstanding payments of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd, including staff salaries with CPF contributions, mortgage loans for the Three Properties totalling $19,331.62, and an outstanding $2,000,864.89 needed to complete the purchase of The Makena condominium.

On 8 July 2011, an email was sent by Daniel Long or company staff to Rudolf Johanes Merukh or a representative of the deceased’s Indonesian companies requesting funds for Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
On 14 July 2011, an email was sent by Daniel Long or company staff to Indonesian counterparts appealing for funds for Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
On 19 July 2011, an email was sent by Daniel Long or company staff to Indonesian counterparts appealing for funds for Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
On 21 July 2011, an email was sent by Daniel Long or company staff to Indonesian counterparts appealing for funds for Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
On 22 July 2011, the seller of The Makena condominium issued a 21-day notice instructing completion of the purchase to Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.
By mid-August 2011, the 21-day completion notice for The Makena purchase lapsed and the sale was not completed.

On 28 July 2011, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh requesting that the executor of the estate sign a board circular resolution authorising the sale of all properties and cars owned by Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and other Merukh companies, settlement of all debts, and closure or disposal of all Merukh companies in Singapore.

On 28 July 2011, a draft board circular resolution was prepared for Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and other Merukh companies to sell all properties and cars, settle debts, and authorise closure or disposal of the companies.
On 13 July 2011, creditors who financed Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd’s car purchases contacted the company to repossess cars that had been purchased for use by various pastors.
Around July 2011, Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd sought payment from Daniel Long as guarantor for a shortfall arising from depreciation of repossessed cars of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd.
During July and August 2011, staff salaries and CPF contributions for employees of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd remained unpaid.
During July and August 2011, authorities issued warnings to Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd about penalties and criminal sanctions for failure to pay CPF contributions.

On 20 August 2011, United Overseas Bank sent a final reminder to Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd demanding payment of outstanding instalments for the Three Properties and warning of possible legal action.

From 5 July 2011 onwards, Daniel Long repeatedly emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh requesting funds to meet mortgage instalments for the Three Properties and warning that auction sales could result in low prices and losses to Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.

On 18 July 2011, Daniel Long and Rudolf Johanes Merukh had a phone conversation in which Rudolf stated that the Merukh family had decided to sell all Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies under Daniel Long’s charge.
On 20 July 2011, Rudolf Johanes Merukh sent a text message to Daniel Long confirming the instruction to sell all Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies.

On 3 August 2011, Daniel Long emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh stating that he had appointed a property agent to market the Three Properties and warning of the need to sell quickly to avoid repossession and auction by the bank.
On 3 August 2011, Daniel Long appointed a property agent to market the Three Properties owned by Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.

By 3 August 2011, Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd had made offers to purchase the Bayshore property for $1,800,000 and the Kitchener property for $1,500,000, which were slightly below their purchase and current market prices.

On 3 August 2011, Daniel Long emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh explaining that the property market was weak due to anticipated government cooling measures and financial market instability and asked for comments on Motor-Way’s offers.
On 15 August 2011, company staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh urgently requesting signature of the board resolution and transfer of funds to meet staff salaries and CPF payments.
On 17 August 2011, company staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh again urgently requesting signature of the board resolution and transfer of funds.
On 22 August 2011, company staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh again urgently requesting signature of the board resolution and transfer of funds.

On 25 August 2011, company staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh again urgently requesting signature of the board resolution and transfer of funds, stating that staff salaries and CPF payments then totalled $117,982.

On 25 August 2011, Daniel Long sent a formal letter to Rudolf Johanes Merukh stating that he intended to strike off all solvent Merukh companies in Singapore and hoped to do the same with Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd and Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd after resolving outstanding loan and credit issues.

On 25 August 2011, Daniel Long informed Rudolf Johanes Merukh that he was trying to find a buyer for at least one of the Three Properties to raise funds for debts, salaries and CPF contributions, but that the market was slow and no offers were then available.

On 25 August 2011, Daniel Long emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh a copy of United Overseas Bank’s final reminder dated 20 August 2011 and a letter from Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd’s lawyers claiming against him as guarantor.
On 30 August 2011, company staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh again urgently requesting signature of the board resolution and transfer of funds.

On 31 August 2011, Daniel Long emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh noting that more than a week had passed since his previous email about outstanding employees’ salaries and documentation needed to sell the Three Properties and a Toyota Wish car to settle debts, salaries and CPF contributions.

By 31 August 2011, staff salaries and CPF contributions for employees of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and related companies remained unpaid, and mortgage instalments for the Three Properties were in arrears.
On 2 September 2011, Daniel Long received an offer to purchase the Bayshore property at a price higher than its original purchase price.
On 2 September 2011, Daniel Long emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh stating that he planned to grant an option to purchase the Bayshore property based on the received offer.
On 5 September 2011, Daniel Long, on behalf of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd, granted an option to purchase the Bayshore property.
On 6 September 2011, Daniel Long, on behalf of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd, granted an option to purchase the Kitchener property.
On 6 September 2011, Daniel Long emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh attaching copies of the options to purchase for the Bayshore and Kitchener properties.

On 8 September 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh wrote to Daniel Long stating that the estate did not want the Three Properties to be sold for less than $2,000,000 for the Bayshore property, $1,700,000 for the Kitchener property, and $1,200,000 for The Raintree.
On 9 September 2011, Daniel Long, on behalf of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd, granted an option to purchase The Raintree.

On 19 September 2011, lawyers for the estate issued a legal notice to Daniel Long directing him to cancel the options for the Three Properties or, if already exercised, to deposit sale proceeds into a bank account of Nunik Elizabeth Merukh and stating that legal action for embezzlement of the Three Properties and sale proceeds was contemplated.

On 26 September 2011, lawyers for the estate issued a second legal notice to Daniel Long repeating directions regarding cancellation of options or deposit of sale proceeds and referring to possible criminal and civil action.
On 29 September 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh emailed Daniel Long requesting a meeting to discuss the sale of the Three Properties.
After 29 September 2011, Daniel Long did not agree to a meeting requested by Nunik Elizabeth Merukh to discuss the sale of the Three Properties.
After 29 September 2011, Daniel Long did not sign a circular resolution to appoint Nunik Elizabeth Merukh to the board of directors of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.

By the end of September 2011, the options to purchase for the Three Properties had been granted with sale prices of $1,917,500 for the Bayshore property, $1,551,000 for the Kitchener property, and $1,190,000 for The Raintree.

By the end of September 2011, the sale prices for the Three Properties exceeded their original purchase prices of $1,916,200 for the Bayshore property, $1,550,000 for the Kitchener property, and $1,155,000 for The Raintree.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Daniel Long’s pre-dispute conduct in deciding to grant options to purchase and sell the Three Properties, and in managing the company’s financial pressures after the shareholder’s death, exposed him to potential liability to the company or to the estate under company law.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 391 of the Companies Act, a court may relieve a company officer from liability for negligence, default, of duty or of trust if the officer acted honestly and reasonably and, having regard to all the circumstances including those connected with the appointment, ought fairly to be excused. Section 160 of the Companies Act requires directors to obtain approval in general meeting before carrying into effect proposals for disposing of the whole or substantially the whole of the company’s undertaking or property.
Party_Details.Application: Daniel Long, as sole director after 22 June 2011, managed Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd in a context of unpaid staff salaries, mortgage arrears on the Three Properties, and creditor pressure, while repeatedly seeking instructions and funds from the deceased’s family and representatives. He obtained instructions from a family member to sell the Singapore properties and cars and to wind down the companies, initiated a board circular resolution to authorise sales and closure, and appointed an agent to market the Three Properties. Facing a final reminder from the mortgagee bank and continuing lack of funds, he granted options to purchase the Three Properties in early September 2011 at prices above their original purchase prices and close to contemporaneous valuations, before receiving a later communication from the estate specifying minimum desired prices. The estate’s lawyers then issued legal notices directing cancellation of options or deposit of proceeds and referring to possible legal action, and Daniel Long did not agree to a requested meeting or sign a resolution to appoint the estate representative to the board. These events formed the factual basis for potential claims concerning his decisions on timing, pricing, and authorisation of the property disposals and his compliance with statutory corporate approval requirements.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge characterised the application as one for prospective relief under section 391(2) of the Companies Act and analysed two main questions: whether section 391 could apply to proceedings brought by persons other than the company, and whether, if applicable, the plaintiff had acted honestly, reasonably, and ought fairly to be excused. After reviewing English and Australian authorities and the legislative history of the relieving provision and related indemnity provisions, the judge adopted the narrower English position that section 391 operates within the context of the company’s relationship with its officers and does not extend to claims brought by third parties such as the estate or its representative. The judge therefore declined to extend prospective relief to potential proceedings by persons other than the company. The judge nonetheless considered, for completeness, whether relief should be granted in respect of potential claims by the company. Applying the statutory criteria, the judge found that the plaintiff’s conduct in granting the options and effecting the sales was not motivated by personal gain, did not involve concealment comparable to prior local cases, and occurred against a background of persistent efforts to obtain instructions and approvals from the deceased’s family and representatives. The judge accepted that the plaintiff acted honestly, without moral turpitude, and that his omissions, including failure to obtain prior general meeting approval, were genuine oversights. In assessing reasonableness, the judge took into account the plaintiff’s lack of business experience, his appointment based on personal trust rather than commercial expertise, the financial pressures from mortgage arrears, staff salary and CPF obligations, and creditor claims, as well as the soft property market and the fact that the achieved sale prices were close to independent valuations and above original purchase prices. The judge concluded that the plaintiff acted as he would have in his own affairs and that his conduct was reasonable in the circumstances. On the fairness limb, the judge considered the seriousness of the alleged contraventions, the absence of flagrant or deceitful behaviour, the limited adverse financial impact on the estate given the sale prices, and the plaintiff’s position and responsibilities. The judge determined that it would be fair to excuse the plaintiff from liability to the company for any negligence, default, breach of duty or breach of trust arising from his decisions to grant the options and complete the sales of the Three Properties. The court therefore granted the plaintiff full prospective relief under section 391(2) as against claims brought by or on behalf of the company, while refusing relief in respect of potential claims by the defendant or other third parties.

Party_Details.Name: Merukh Nunik Elizabeth (personal representative of the estate of Merukh Jusuf, deceased)

Facts.Text: On 22 June 2011, Dr Jusuf Merukh died, and his daughter Nunik Elizabeth Merukh acted as personal representative of his estate.

By 30 April 2010, Dr Jusuf Merukh was the sole shareholder of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and had provided all funds for the purchase of the Three Properties through transfers from his Indonesian bank account.
By 30 April 2010, Dr Jusuf Merukh had incorporated 14 companies in Singapore, each wholly owned by him, including Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.

On 18 July 2011, during a phone conversation with Daniel Long, Rudolf Johanes Merukh, the eldest son of the deceased, stated that the Merukh family had decided to sell all Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies under Daniel Long’s charge.
On 20 July 2011, Rudolf Johanes Merukh sent a text message to Daniel Long confirming the family’s decision to sell all Three Properties and cars in Singapore and to wind down the Singapore companies.

On 28 July 2011, Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd staff emailed Rudolf Johanes Merukh requesting that the executor of the estate sign a board circular resolution authorising the sale of all properties and cars owned by Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd and other Merukh companies, settlement of all debts, and closure or disposal of all Merukh companies in Singapore.
On 3 August 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh requested and obtained from United Overseas Bank a six-month grace period for payment of outstanding instalments on the Three Properties.

On 8 September 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh wrote to Daniel Long stating that the estate did not want the Bayshore property sold for less than $2,000,000, the Kitchener property for less than $1,700,000, and The Raintree for less than $1,200,000.

On 19 September 2011, lawyers for the estate issued a legal notice to Daniel Long directing him to cancel the options for the Three Properties or, if already exercised, to deposit the sale proceeds into a bank account of Nunik Elizabeth Merukh and referring to possible criminal and civil action concerning the Three Properties and sale proceeds.

On 26 September 2011, lawyers for the estate issued a second legal notice to Daniel Long repeating directions regarding cancellation of options or deposit of sale proceeds and referring to possible legal action.
On 29 September 2011, Nunik Elizabeth Merukh emailed Daniel Long requesting a meeting to discuss the sale of the Three Properties.
After 29 September 2011, a circular resolution was prepared to appoint Nunik Elizabeth Merukh to the board of directors of Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd, which Daniel Long did not sign.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the estate, through its personal representative, could pursue claims against Daniel Long in relation to the sale of the Three Properties and whether section 391 of the Companies Act could affect such claims.
Party_Details.Rule: Section 391 of the Companies Act provides for court-ordered relief from liability for company officers in proceedings for negligence, default, of duty or of trust, but its scope is interpreted by reference to its legislative history and its relationship with statutory provisions restricting indemnities, and may be confined to actions brought by the company. Section 160 of the Companies Act requires shareholder approval in general meeting for disposals of the whole or substantially the whole of a company’s undertaking or property.
Party_Details.Application: The estate, represented by Nunik Elizabeth Merukh, funded the acquisition of the Three Properties through the deceased’s personal funds and communicated desired minimum sale prices and instructions regarding cancellation of options or handling of sale proceeds after Daniel Long had granted options to purchase. The estate’s lawyers issued legal notices referring to potential criminal and civil proceedings and directing that proceeds be deposited into an account of the representative. The representative also obtained a grace period from the mortgagee bank and sought to join the company’s board. These steps formed the basis for potential claims by the estate or its representative concerning the timing, pricing, and authorisation of the property sales and the handling of sale proceeds, and raised the question whether statutory relief under section 391 could apply where proceedings were initiated by a party other than the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge examined whether section 391 of the Companies Act could extend to proceedings brought by persons other than the company, such as the estate represented by Nunik Elizabeth Merukh. After analysing English and Australian case law and the legislative history of the relieving and indemnity provisions, the judge concluded that section 391 operates as a balancing mechanism within company law and is confined to liabilities owed in relation to the company. The judge preferred the English position that relief is not available against third-party claims and held that section 391 does not apply to actions brought by the estate or its representative. The judge therefore declined to grant prospective relief to Daniel Long in respect of potential proceedings by the defendant, while separately granting relief only in relation to potential claims by the company.

Party_Details.Name: Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd (intervener)

Facts.Text: Around July 2011, Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd, as a creditor of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd, repossessed cars that had been financed for use by various pastors.
Around July 2011, Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd sought payment from Daniel Long as personal guarantor for a shortfall arising from depreciation of repossessed cars of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd.
On 25 August 2011, Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd’s lawyers sent a letter to Daniel Long claiming payment under his guarantee for Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd’s outstanding debts.
By 3 August 2011, Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd had made offers to purchase the Bayshore property for $1,800,000 and the Kitchener property for $1,500,000 from Merukh Singapore Properties Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: How Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd’s creditor position and offers to purchase two of the Three Properties related to Daniel Long’s decisions on property disposals and his potential exposure to liability.
Party_Details.Rule: Company officers may face personal exposure where they provide guarantees for company debts, and creditor pressure and purchase offers can form part of the factual context in assessing decisions on asset disposals and potential liability under company law standards such as section 391 of the Companies Act.
Party_Details.Application: Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd, as a creditor of Merukh Republic Auto Pte Ltd, repossessed vehicles and pursued Daniel Long under his personal guarantee for any shortfall, while also making purchase offers for the Bayshore and Kitchener properties at prices slightly below their purchase and market values. These creditor actions and offers formed part of the financial pressures and available options considered by Daniel Long when deciding whether and how quickly to sell the Three Properties, and were the overall context in which his potential liability as a director was later assessed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge treated Motor-Way Credit Pte Ltd’s role primarily as part of the factual background illustrating the financial pressures on Daniel Long, including repossession of vehicles, pursuit of the plaintiff under a personal guarantee, and offers to purchase two of the Three Properties. The court did not focus on independent liability issues concerning Motor-Way but considered its actions as relevant context in evaluating the plaintiff’s conduct and in determining whether to grant relief under section 391 in relation to potential claims by the company.
